medical.title: New hand sensors turn post-stroke rehab into an on-screen game
medical.summary: New paper-thin piezoelectric patch sensors designed for at-home stroke rehabilitation may soon revolutionize post-stroke care by offering potential accessible, efficient rehabilitation and improving patient outcomes through self-motivated health care and entertainment.

medical.title: How Aboriginal nations fought 1830s smallpox
medical.summary: As Aboriginal nations mounted a series of coordinated and strategic campaigns to defend Country against invading settlers, the smallpox epidemic spread across the southeast from 1830 to 1832.

medical.title: PET imaging reveals synaptic loss in multiple sclerosis across preclinical and human studies
medical.summary: A new PET imaging approach that measures synaptic density in the spinal cord provides a quantitative way to assess the brain's functional wiring in patients with multiple sclerosis. With this personalized information, physicians can monitor disease progression and evaluate whether new treatments are working to protect or restore these critical connections. This research was presented at the Society of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging's 2026 Annual Meeting.

medical.title: AI 'digital twins' are transforming heart care but will they work for women?
medical.summary: AI-powered digital twin technology could transform how doctors understand and treat heart disease. But if the medical data used to build these virtual models overlook biological differences between women and men, the promise of truly personalized medicine may remain incomplete.

medical.title: Immunotherapy added to radiation therapy boosts survival in localized prostate cancer
medical.summary: Results of a multicenter clinical trial found that adding the investigational adenoviral-based viral immunotherapy aglatimagene besadenovec (alglatimagene, CAN-2409) to standard radiation therapy improved disease-free survival for patients with intermediate- or high-risk localized prostate cancer. The study was led by researchers at the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins Kimmel Cancer Center, Department of Radiation Oncology and Molecular Radiation Sciences, and Brady Urological Institute. Findings from the study were published June 1 in The Lancet Oncology.

medical.title: Fathers' diet before conception could significantly affect fetal growth and placenta development
medical.summary: What fathers eat before conception could significantly influence fetal growth and placenta development, new research in mice suggests. A team of scientists from the University of Sheffield, found that although dietary differences had no major effect on fertility, both high-fat "Western-style" diets and low-protein diets in males altered key biological processes in the placenta after fertilization. The findings, published in eLife, add to growing evidence that paternal health and nutrition before conception may play an important role in early development.

medical.title: Deep brain stimulation boosts myelination and shifts brain networks linked to depression
medical.summary: Researchers from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have uncovered the first direct evidence that deep brain stimulation (DBS) can remodel white matter pathways in the brain and alter communication across large-scale neural networks, revealing a previously unrecognized mechanism that may explain how the therapy helps patients recover from severe depression. The study, published June 1 in Nature Neuroscience, provides critical insight into the biological basis of DBS, an emerging therapy for treatment-resistant depression and other neuropsychiatric disorders.

medical.title: Poor sleep, night shift work linked to higher risk of osteoarthritis
medical.summary: Researchers at WashU Medicine found that adults who regularly experience short or poor-quality sleep, as well as those who work night shifts, face a significantly higher risk of developing osteoarthritis and requiring hip or knee replacement surgery.

medical.title: Real-time calibration helps fitness trackers better match lab-tested exercise measurements
medical.summary: Researchers have developed an enhanced wearable motion-tracking system that could improve the accuracy of fitness trackers used to monitor exercise and training. The team provides details in an article published in the International Journal of Data Mining and Bioinformatics.

medical.title: Irradiation may help CAR-T cell therapy work better against solid tumors
medical.summary: Researchers from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have discovered a promising new way to improve CAR-T cell therapy for solid tumors such as lung cancer and melanoma. The study, published in Nature Cancer, found that focused irradiation, a targeted therapy that delivers high-energy beams to stun rapidly growing cells such as cancer, can help CAR-T cells survive longer and work more effectively inside tumors.

medical.title: Researchers say daylight saving time may worsen cognitive, psychological problems
medical.summary: Daylight saving time isn't just a seasonal inconvenience—it may also pose significant neuropsychological risks for the hundreds of millions of people worldwide who experience biannual clock shifts, especially those living with chronic mental illnesses. That's according to a major new study by a team of researchers from New Mexico State University's College of Health, Education and Social Transformation and the University of Nevada, Las Vegas's Kirk Kerkorian School of Medicine.

medical.title: Study links childhood trauma and substance use to higher rates of depression
medical.summary: A new study from the Texas A&M University School of Public Health found that childhood trauma, such as abuse, neglect or household instability, and using multiple substances at the same time—particularly cannabis and stimulants—are strongly linked to experiencing depressive symptoms among college students.

medical.title: New practice guidance available for treatment of enlarged prostate
medical.summary: The Society of Interventional Radiology (SIR) has published new practice guidance for the use of prostatic artery embolization (PAE) to treat enlarged prostate (also called benign prostatic hyperplasia or BPH). The guidance appears in the Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology to coincide with the start of Men's Health Month in June.

medical.title: Largest study of knee osteoarthritis tissue reveals core biological pathways underlying the disease
medical.summary: A major international study led by researchers at the Kennedy Institute of Rheumatology at the University of Oxford has found that osteoarthritis (OA)—the most common form of arthritis worldwide—is not a collection of separate diseases, as many scientists had previously speculated, but rather a single condition with common core underlying biological pathways.

medical.title: Study on circulating tumor DNA shows clear benefits
medical.summary: After surgery for colon cancer, many patients face the question of whether follow-up chemotherapy is necessary to prevent a possible relapse. The decision is particularly difficult in so-called Stage II, the intermediate-risk group. Although around one in five untreated patients suffers a relapse, adjuvant chemotherapy places a significant and, in some cases, unnecessary burden on many patients. A clinical study led by Dresden University Hospital now provides important insights for a robust basis for decision-making.

medical.title: Predicting the financial strain of cancer
medical.summary: Researchers at MUSC Hollings Cancer Center have developed a machine learning tool to identify cancer patients who may be at high risk for financial toxicity—the financial stress and hardship that can accompany a cancer diagnosis and treatment. The study brought together several investigators from the Hollings Cancer Prevention and Control Research Program, reflecting the project's focus on cancer outcomes, survivorship and care delivery.

medical.title: CRISPR model links faulty collagen to brain microbleeds tied to memory decline
medical.summary: Millions of older adults have tiny brain hemorrhages called cerebral microbleeds, which are strongly associated with dementia, cognitive decline, and stroke. However, their precise molecular mechanisms have remained unclear, largely because of the lack of suitable animal models that isolate this condition from other confounding pathologies. A study published in the journal Brain helps fill this critical gap.

medical.title: Shrunken genomes may drive rapid cancer evolution and poor outcomes
medical.summary: Researchers at Trinity have found that a largely overlooked form of cancer evolution, which drives aggressive disease and poor patient outcomes, is far more widespread than previously thought. Comparing chromosome patterns in these tumors, the team also developed a new method for accurately distinguishing between two leukemias, which could provide information to better guide patient treatment decisions.

medical.title: Stressing about your baby's growth check? Here's what you need to know
medical.summary: If you've ever taken your child to a maternal, child and family health nurse for a growth check, you might have felt a mix of curiosity and anxiety.

science.title: Q&A: How better climate data supports smarter environmental decisions
science.summary: Accurate measurements are the foundation of effective environmental management and decision-making. Through advanced monitoring networks and computer models, Ken Davis, professor of meteorology and atmospheric science in Penn State's College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, and his research group are helping scientists, communities, and policymakers better understand urban heat, greenhouse gas emissions, and air quality.

science.title: Faster lower-cost PFAS testing could reshape how US drinking water is monitored
science.summary: A new investigation from the University of Kansas improves detection of PFAS, a family of so-called "forever chemicals" in drinking water supplies. The method, which can measure trace pollution levels of PFAS in water more quickly and inexpensively than current techniques, was recently detailed in the journal PLOS Water.

science.title: Proteins can be selectively controlled with radio waves
science.summary: In a significant advance in biological quantum sensing, a research team led by the Technical University of Munich (TUM) has discovered and tested a new mechanism of action in which proteins can be controlled with radio waves. In doing so, they influence a sensitive quantum state known as spin and make it visible via light. In the future, such findings could help detect and even direct biochemical processes in cells simply from the outside using radio waves.

science.title: NASA Space Roboticist Challenge
science.summary: The Fly Foundational Robots (FFR) mission will launch a robotic arm, with seven degrees of freedom, to low Earth orbit. NASA is opening access to the robotic arm to a select group of U.S. researchers — principal investigators, post-doctoral researchers, professors, and highly qualified graduate students — who have a compelling experiment and the capability […]

science.title: Municipal partnership systems and mental health among sexual minorities in Japan: A nationwide analysis
science.summary: Although same-sex marriage has not been legalized nationally in Japan, various municipal governments have independently introduced partnership certification systems for same-sex couples.

science.title: Embryonic tissues can behave like fluids or solids to reshape cell fate signals
science.summary: Embryonic development is one of the most dynamic biological processes in nature. Cells and tissues organize and reorganize themselves following incredibly precise patterns, while remaining flexible and robust. Scientists are increasingly probing the role the physical properties of embryonic tissues—such as rigidity or stiffness—play in this process.

science.title: France follows England in measuring hottest spring on record
science.summary: France experienced its hottest spring on record, the country's weather service said Tuesday, after an exceptional early heat wave that also broke seasonal highs in England and Wales.

science.title: Amazon rainforest emits new stress-defense molecules during El Niño drought
science.summary: The Amazon rainforest responded to the most severe drought ever recorded in the basin with an unexpected defense mechanism. Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Chemistry in Mainz, Germany, found that during and after the intense 2023–2024 El Niño cycle, the most intense drought ever recorded in the region, vegetation significantly changed its chemical emissions to cope with environmental stress. The study was published in Communications Earth & Environment.

science.title: First human SMUG1 atomic snapshots reveal how cells repair DNA
science.summary: Researchers have captured the first atomic structures of human SMUG1, an enzyme that helps cells repair damaged DNA. The findings provide new insight into how cells recognize and remove harmful DNA bases, and may support future efforts to develop drugs that target this DNA repair pathway.

science.title: Rare meteorite provides evidence of giant early planet
science.summary: Four-and-a-half billion years ago, a massive world—possibly as big as the moon or even Mars—orbited our sun before crashing into another celestial body and shattering into rubble. Now, in a paper published in the journal Earth and Planetary Science Letters, scientists report the first definitive evidence that this lost planetary embryo (protoplanet) existed. Its unique geological makeup challenges long-held assumptions about how planets evolve.

science.title: Clean drinking water gaps linked to hunger and unsafe food worldwide
science.summary: A new global study has found that people without access to clean drinking water are significantly more likely to experience food insecurity and food safety threats, underscoring the urgent need for coordinated global action to address these issues together.

science.title: Life in the ancient Arctic: Tiny teeth of newly discovered species suggest it was a cradle of mammalian evolution
science.summary: A fossil mammal tooth smaller than a grain of rice does not announce itself loudly. It must be hard won from sediment and stone. Then, under a microscope, it reveals itself—no longer just a speck of blackness but a surface of cusps, ridges, and worn edges.

science.title: Lab evolution recreates COVID's path to omicron in months, reveals key conditions
science.summary: A key step in the origin of many pandemics occurs when an animal-borne virus infects humans and then evolves to spread more efficiently from person to person. That is why scientists and physicians keep a close watch on viruses that could jump from animals to humans, such as emerging strains of avian flu and bat coronaviruses, as well as viruses that have already crossed into humans but, for now, spread poorly among people, such as hantavirus and Ebola.

science.title: Single cell transforms into cannibalistic 'supergiant,' swallowing its clones whole
science.summary: Researchers at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (RPI) have discovered a microscopic organism that can transform into a cannibalistic "supergiant" that drastically changes size, shape, and behavior, and abandons filter-feeding to hunt and consume their genetically identical relatives.

science.title: Global supply chains keep workers poor: Three case studies show how the cycle can be broken
science.summary: Globally, about 1 in 5 people in jobs live in poverty. A key reason lies in how global supply chains are organized. From agriculture to tourism, many jobs are embedded in systems that keep wages low, even as they generate value for international markets.

science.title: Kamo'oalewa asteroid's lunar origin challenged ahead of Tianwen-2 arrival
science.summary: China's Tianwen-2 sample-return mission is well on its way to its target, an asteroid called Kamo'oalewa. The spacecraft left Earth in May 2025 and should return in late 2027 with samples of a space rock that scientists had assumed originated from the moon. However, a new study published in Nature Communications suggests that we may be mistaken about the asteroid's origin.

science.title: 'Genetic brakes' reveal how embryos shape their limbs
science.summary: Canadian scientists have made a significant advance in understanding the mechanisms that enable embryos to properly form their limbs, thanks to new research led by Université de Montréal medical professor Marie Kmita at the Montreal Clinical Research Institute (IRCM). In findings published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Kmita and her team highlight the crucial role of certain molecular systems that act as true "genetic brakes," ensuring that development proceeds correctly.

science.title: Fluorescent nanosensor detects key gut biomarker in minutes for faster testing
science.summary: A research collaboration has developed a novel fluorescent nanosensor capable of rapidly detecting indole-3-propionic acid (IPA), an emerging biomarker linked to gut health and disease. The breakthrough is described in the team's paper, "Fluorescent Nanosensor for Indole-3-Propionic Acid Detection in Gut Health Monitoring," published in the journal Advanced Healthcare Materials.

science.title: Redesigning an elusive bacterial enzyme into an efficient green catalyst
science.summary: Industrial oxidation chemistry is a cornerstone of modern manufacturing, accounting for nearly one-third of all chemical industrial processes. While essential for making pharmaceuticals, dyes, and many specialty chemicals, industrial oxidation typically relies on high-temperature, high-pressure processes involving toxic oxidizing agents. This has motivated scientists to look into cytochrome P450 monooxygenases (P450s) as a compelling alternative.

science.title: Extreme weather is making Antarctic research harder, but new technology is providing some answers
science.summary: When you think of Antarctica, you might imagine a stark, otherworldly continent of endless, white ice, with the only sound being the wind punctuated by the crack of a glacier calving in the distance.

science.title: Look Up!
science.summary: Astronauts Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) and Jack Hathaway of NASA, both Expedition 74 flight engineers, look out a window in the cupola, monitoring the automated approach and docking of the SpaceX Dragon cargo spacecraft to the International Space Station on May 17, 2026. The orbital outpost was soaring 259 miles above the […]

science.title: NASA Testing Wastewater Treatment Facility for Future Moon Base
science.summary: A mobile wastewater treatment system built at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida that can help prepare for long-duration missions on the Moon and Mars departed the spaceport and arrived at the University of North Dakota in Grand Forks. Graduate students at the university will test the technology under conditions designed to closely mimic the […]

science.title: Be a Clump Scout and Help Reveal Secrets of Stellar Nurseries
science.summary: Help identify star-forming clumps in galaxy images, and help train machines to do the same.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Ea s-a lăudat că a făcut cu propriile mâini o colivă de 1,8 tone, pentru a comemora memoria unor eroi ai neamului, dar cine s-a uitat cu atenție la imagini a putut descoperi imediat impostura. mix_items.title: New hand sensors turn post-stroke rehab into an on-screen game
mix_items.summary: New paper-thin piezoelectric patch sensors designed for at-home stroke rehabilitation may soon revolutionize post-stroke care by offering potential accessible, efficient rehabilitation and improving patient outcomes through self-motivated health care and entertainment.

mix_items.title: Q&A: How better climate data supports smarter environmental decisions
mix_items.summary: Accurate measurements are the foundation of effective environmental management and decision-making. Through advanced monitoring networks and computer models, Ken Davis, professor of meteorology and atmospheric science in Penn State's College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, and his research group are helping scientists, communities, and policymakers better understand urban heat, greenhouse gas emissions, and air quality.

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Faster lower-cost PFAS testing could reshape how US drinking water is monitored
mix_items.summary: A new investigation from the University of Kansas improves detection of PFAS, a family of so-called "forever chemicals" in drinking water supplies. The method, which can measure trace pollution levels of PFAS in water more quickly and inexpensively than current techniques, was recently detailed in the journal PLOS Water.

mix_items.title: How Aboriginal nations fought 1830s smallpox
mix_items.summary: As Aboriginal nations mounted a series of coordinated and strategic campaigns to defend Country against invading settlers, the smallpox epidemic spread across the southeast from 1830 to 1832.

Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: PET imaging reveals synaptic loss in multiple sclerosis across preclinical and human studies
mix_items.summary: A new PET imaging approach that measures synaptic density in the spinal cord provides a quantitative way to assess the brain's functional wiring in patients with multiple sclerosis. With this personalized information, physicians can monitor disease progression and evaluate whether new treatments are working to protect or restore these critical connections. This research was presented at the Society of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging's 2026 Annual Meeting.

mix_items.title: AI 'digital twins' are transforming heart care but will they work for women?
mix_items.summary: AI-powered digital twin technology could transform how doctors understand and treat heart disease. But if the medical data used to build these virtual models overlook biological differences between women and men, the promise of truly personalized medicine may remain incomplete.

mix_items.title: Proteins can be selectively controlled with radio waves
mix_items.summary: In a significant advance in biological quantum sensing, a research team led by the Technical University of Munich (TUM) has discovered and tested a new mechanism of action in which proteins can be controlled with radio waves. In doing so, they influence a sensitive quantum state known as spin and make it visible via light. In the future, such findings could help detect and even direct biochemical processes in cells simply from the outside using radio waves.

mix_items.title: NASA Space Roboticist Challenge
mix_items.summary: The Fly Foundational Robots (FFR) mission will launch a robotic arm, with seven degrees of freedom, to low Earth orbit. NASA is opening access to the robotic arm to a select group of U.S. researchers — principal investigators, post-doctoral researchers, professors, and highly qualified graduate students — who have a compelling experiment and the capability […]

mix_items.title: Municipal partnership systems and mental health among sexual minorities in Japan: A nationwide analysis
mix_items.summary: Although same-sex marriage has not been legalized nationally in Japan, various municipal governments have independently introduced partnership certification systems for same-sex couples.

mix_items.title: Immunotherapy added to radiation therapy boosts survival in localized prostate cancer
mix_items.summary: Results of a multicenter clinical trial found that adding the investigational adenoviral-based viral immunotherapy aglatimagene besadenovec (alglatimagene, CAN-2409) to standard radiation therapy improved disease-free survival for patients with intermediate- or high-risk localized prostate cancer. The study was led by researchers at the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins Kimmel Cancer Center, Department of Radiation Oncology and Molecular Radiation Sciences, and Brady Urological Institute. Findings from the study were published June 1 in The Lancet Oncology.

mix_items.title: Fathers' diet before conception could significantly affect fetal growth and placenta development
mix_items.summary: What fathers eat before conception could significantly influence fetal growth and placenta development, new research in mice suggests. A team of scientists from the University of Sheffield, found that although dietary differences had no major effect on fertility, both high-fat "Western-style" diets and low-protein diets in males altered key biological processes in the placenta after fertilization. The findings, published in eLife, add to growing evidence that paternal health and nutrition before conception may play an important role in early development.

mix_items.title: Embryonic tissues can behave like fluids or solids to reshape cell fate signals
mix_items.summary: Embryonic development is one of the most dynamic biological processes in nature. Cells and tissues organize and reorganize themselves following incredibly precise patterns, while remaining flexible and robust. Scientists are increasingly probing the role the physical properties of embryonic tissues—such as rigidity or stiffness—play in this process.

mix_items.title: France follows England in measuring hottest spring on record
mix_items.summary: France experienced its hottest spring on record, the country's weather service said Tuesday, after an exceptional early heat wave that also broke seasonal highs in England and Wales.

mix_items.title: Amazon rainforest emits new stress-defense molecules during El Niño drought
mix_items.summary: The Amazon rainforest responded to the most severe drought ever recorded in the basin with an unexpected defense mechanism. Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Chemistry in Mainz, Germany, found that during and after the intense 2023–2024 El Niño cycle, the most intense drought ever recorded in the region, vegetation significantly changed its chemical emissions to cope with environmental stress. The study was published in Communications Earth & Environment.

mix_items.title: Deep brain stimulation boosts myelination and shifts brain networks linked to depression
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have uncovered the first direct evidence that deep brain stimulation (DBS) can remodel white matter pathways in the brain and alter communication across large-scale neural networks, revealing a previously unrecognized mechanism that may explain how the therapy helps patients recover from severe depression. The study, published June 1 in Nature Neuroscience, provides critical insight into the biological basis of DBS, an emerging therapy for treatment-resistant depression and other neuropsychiatric disorders.

mix_items.title: Poor sleep, night shift work linked to higher risk of osteoarthritis
mix_items.summary: Researchers at WashU Medicine found that adults who regularly experience short or poor-quality sleep, as well as those who work night shifts, face a significantly higher risk of developing osteoarthritis and requiring hip or knee replacement surgery.

mix_items.title: First human SMUG1 atomic snapshots reveal how cells repair DNA
mix_items.summary: Researchers have captured the first atomic structures of human SMUG1, an enzyme that helps cells repair damaged DNA. The findings provide new insight into how cells recognize and remove harmful DNA bases, and may support future efforts to develop drugs that target this DNA repair pathway.

mix_items.title: Rare meteorite provides evidence of giant early planet
mix_items.summary: Four-and-a-half billion years ago, a massive world—possibly as big as the moon or even Mars—orbited our sun before crashing into another celestial body and shattering into rubble. Now, in a paper published in the journal Earth and Planetary Science Letters, scientists report the first definitive evidence that this lost planetary embryo (protoplanet) existed. Its unique geological makeup challenges long-held assumptions about how planets evolve.

mix_items.title: Real-time calibration helps fitness trackers better match lab-tested exercise measurements
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed an enhanced wearable motion-tracking system that could improve the accuracy of fitness trackers used to monitor exercise and training. The team provides details in an article published in the International Journal of Data Mining and Bioinformatics.

mix_items.title: Irradiation may help CAR-T cell therapy work better against solid tumors
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have discovered a promising new way to improve CAR-T cell therapy for solid tumors such as lung cancer and melanoma. The study, published in Nature Cancer, found that focused irradiation, a targeted therapy that delivers high-energy beams to stun rapidly growing cells such as cancer, can help CAR-T cells survive longer and work more effectively inside tumors.

mix_items.title: Clean drinking water gaps linked to hunger and unsafe food worldwide
mix_items.summary: A new global study has found that people without access to clean drinking water are significantly more likely to experience food insecurity and food safety threats, underscoring the urgent need for coordinated global action to address these issues together.

mix_items.title: Researchers say daylight saving time may worsen cognitive, psychological problems
mix_items.summary: Daylight saving time isn't just a seasonal inconvenience—it may also pose significant neuropsychological risks for the hundreds of millions of people worldwide who experience biannual clock shifts, especially those living with chronic mental illnesses. That's according to a major new study by a team of researchers from New Mexico State University's College of Health, Education and Social Transformation and the University of Nevada, Las Vegas's Kirk Kerkorian School of Medicine.

mix_items.title: Study links childhood trauma and substance use to higher rates of depression
mix_items.summary: A new study from the Texas A&M University School of Public Health found that childhood trauma, such as abuse, neglect or household instability, and using multiple substances at the same time—particularly cannabis and stimulants—are strongly linked to experiencing depressive symptoms among college students.

mix_items.title: Life in the ancient Arctic: Tiny teeth of newly discovered species suggest it was a cradle of mammalian evolution
mix_items.summary: A fossil mammal tooth smaller than a grain of rice does not announce itself loudly. It must be hard won from sediment and stone. Then, under a microscope, it reveals itself—no longer just a speck of blackness but a surface of cusps, ridges, and worn edges.

mix_items.title: Lab evolution recreates COVID's path to omicron in months, reveals key conditions
mix_items.summary: A key step in the origin of many pandemics occurs when an animal-borne virus infects humans and then evolves to spread more efficiently from person to person. That is why scientists and physicians keep a close watch on viruses that could jump from animals to humans, such as emerging strains of avian flu and bat coronaviruses, as well as viruses that have already crossed into humans but, for now, spread poorly among people, such as hantavirus and Ebola.

mix_items.title: Single cell transforms into cannibalistic 'supergiant,' swallowing its clones whole
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (RPI) have discovered a microscopic organism that can transform into a cannibalistic "supergiant" that drastically changes size, shape, and behavior, and abandons filter-feeding to hunt and consume their genetically identical relatives.

mix_items.title: New practice guidance available for treatment of enlarged prostate
mix_items.summary: The Society of Interventional Radiology (SIR) has published new practice guidance for the use of prostatic artery embolization (PAE) to treat enlarged prostate (also called benign prostatic hyperplasia or BPH). The guidance appears in the Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology to coincide with the start of Men's Health Month in June.

mix_items.title: Global supply chains keep workers poor: Three case studies show how the cycle can be broken
mix_items.summary: Globally, about 1 in 5 people in jobs live in poverty. A key reason lies in how global supply chains are organized. From agriculture to tourism, many jobs are embedded in systems that keep wages low, even as they generate value for international markets.

mix_items.title: Kamo'oalewa asteroid's lunar origin challenged ahead of Tianwen-2 arrival
mix_items.summary: China's Tianwen-2 sample-return mission is well on its way to its target, an asteroid called Kamo'oalewa. The spacecraft left Earth in May 2025 and should return in late 2027 with samples of a space rock that scientists had assumed originated from the moon. However, a new study published in Nature Communications suggests that we may be mistaken about the asteroid's origin.

mix_items.title: Largest study of knee osteoarthritis tissue reveals core biological pathways underlying the disease
mix_items.summary: A major international study led by researchers at the Kennedy Institute of Rheumatology at the University of Oxford has found that osteoarthritis (OA)—the most common form of arthritis worldwide—is not a collection of separate diseases, as many scientists had previously speculated, but rather a single condition with common core underlying biological pathways.

mix_items.title: Study on circulating tumor DNA shows clear benefits
mix_items.summary: After surgery for colon cancer, many patients face the question of whether follow-up chemotherapy is necessary to prevent a possible relapse. The decision is particularly difficult in so-called Stage II, the intermediate-risk group. Although around one in five untreated patients suffers a relapse, adjuvant chemotherapy places a significant and, in some cases, unnecessary burden on many patients. A clinical study led by Dresden University Hospital now provides important insights for a robust basis for decision-making.

mix_items.title: 'Genetic brakes' reveal how embryos shape their limbs
mix_items.summary: Canadian scientists have made a significant advance in understanding the mechanisms that enable embryos to properly form their limbs, thanks to new research led by Université de Montréal medical professor Marie Kmita at the Montreal Clinical Research Institute (IRCM). In findings published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Kmita and her team highlight the crucial role of certain molecular systems that act as true "genetic brakes," ensuring that development proceeds correctly.

mix_items.title: Predicting the financial strain of cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers at MUSC Hollings Cancer Center have developed a machine learning tool to identify cancer patients who may be at high risk for financial toxicity—the financial stress and hardship that can accompany a cancer diagnosis and treatment. The study brought together several investigators from the Hollings Cancer Prevention and Control Research Program, reflecting the project's focus on cancer outcomes, survivorship and care delivery.

mix_items.title: CRISPR model links faulty collagen to brain microbleeds tied to memory decline
mix_items.summary: Millions of older adults have tiny brain hemorrhages called cerebral microbleeds, which are strongly associated with dementia, cognitive decline, and stroke. However, their precise molecular mechanisms have remained unclear, largely because of the lack of suitable animal models that isolate this condition from other confounding pathologies. A study published in the journal Brain helps fill this critical gap.

mix_items.title: Fluorescent nanosensor detects key gut biomarker in minutes for faster testing
mix_items.summary: A research collaboration has developed a novel fluorescent nanosensor capable of rapidly detecting indole-3-propionic acid (IPA), an emerging biomarker linked to gut health and disease. The breakthrough is described in the team's paper, "Fluorescent Nanosensor for Indole-3-Propionic Acid Detection in Gut Health Monitoring," published in the journal Advanced Healthcare Materials.

mix_items.title: Redesigning an elusive bacterial enzyme into an efficient green catalyst
mix_items.summary: Industrial oxidation chemistry is a cornerstone of modern manufacturing, accounting for nearly one-third of all chemical industrial processes. While essential for making pharmaceuticals, dyes, and many specialty chemicals, industrial oxidation typically relies on high-temperature, high-pressure processes involving toxic oxidizing agents. This has motivated scientists to look into cytochrome P450 monooxygenases (P450s) as a compelling alternative.

mix_items.title: Extreme weather is making Antarctic research harder, but new technology is providing some answers
mix_items.summary: When you think of Antarctica, you might imagine a stark, otherworldly continent of endless, white ice, with the only sound being the wind punctuated by the crack of a glacier calving in the distance.

mix_items.title: Shrunken genomes may drive rapid cancer evolution and poor outcomes
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Trinity have found that a largely overlooked form of cancer evolution, which drives aggressive disease and poor patient outcomes, is far more widespread than previously thought. Comparing chromosome patterns in these tumors, the team also developed a new method for accurately distinguishing between two leukemias, which could provide information to better guide patient treatment decisions.

mix_items.title: Stressing about your baby's growth check? Here's what you need to know
mix_items.summary: If you've ever taken your child to a maternal, child and family health nurse for a growth check, you might have felt a mix of curiosity and anxiety.

mix_items.title: Look Up!
mix_items.summary: Astronauts Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) and Jack Hathaway of NASA, both Expedition 74 flight engineers, look out a window in the cupola, monitoring the automated approach and docking of the SpaceX Dragon cargo spacecraft to the International Space Station on May 17, 2026. The orbital outpost was soaring 259 miles above the […]

mix_items.title: NASA Testing Wastewater Treatment Facility for Future Moon Base
mix_items.summary: A mobile wastewater treatment system built at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida that can help prepare for long-duration missions on the Moon and Mars departed the spaceport and arrived at the University of North Dakota in Grand Forks. Graduate students at the university will test the technology under conditions designed to closely mimic the […]

mix_items.title: Be a Clump Scout and Help Reveal Secrets of Stellar Nurseries
mix_items.summary: Help identify star-forming clumps in galaxy images, and help train machines to do the same.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am verificat contul si el mi-a verificat caracterul.

satire_ro.title: Erată! Șoșoacă nu a făcut o colivă de 1,8 tone, ci o Șoșoacă de 1,8 tone a făcut o colivă
